title: Pashto Poetry & The Attan
description: Discover the powerful two-line Landay poetic form and the traditional Attan dance.
author: Poha Editorial

content.heading: The Power of the Landay
lines: Pashto has a remarkably rich oral poetry tradition. The most unique and powerful form is the 'Landay' (لنډۍ), which means 'short, poisonous snake' in Pashto.

A Landay is an anonymous, two-line folk poem with exactly 22 syllables (9 in the first line, 13 in the second). Historically created and sung by Pashtun women, Landays are fierce, emotional verses addressing love, grief, war, and honor.

content.text: Because they are shared orally and anonymously, Landays represent the collective, uncensored voice of everyday Pashtun life.

content.heading: The Attan: A Dance of Unity
lines: The Attan (اتڼ) is a traditional Pashtun communal dance that has evolved into the national dance of Afghanistan. Originally performed by warriors before going into battle or to celebrate a victory, it is now the centerpiece of weddings and festivals.
Dancers form a circle, following the accelerating rhythm of the Dhol (a double-headed drum) and the melody of the Sorna (a reedy woodwind). The dance can last for hours, requiring immense stamina.

content.alt: A group of men in a circle performing the Attan dance
content.caption: The Attan is performed in a circle, with movements growing increasingly faster and more complex as the drumbeat accelerates.

content.question: What is the syllable structure of a traditional Pashto Landay?
options: 22 syllables (9 in the first line, 13 in the second)
It varies depending on the region
content.explanation: A Landay always consists of exactly 22 syllables: 9 in the first line and 13 in the second. This strict meter allows them to be easily sung to a specific drumbeat.
